topics.title: REST API Design Principles
topics.description: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for HTTP APIs. Resources are nouns (not verbs) in URLs. HTTP methods define the action (GET=read, POST=create, PUT/PATCH=update, DELETE=remove). Idempotency: GET/PUT/DELETE can be retried safely; POST cannot. Status codes communicate outcome (200, 201, 400, 401, 403, 404, 409, 422, 500). Versioning strategies (URL path, header, query param) manage breaking changes. Proper pagination, filtering, and error response shapes make APIs production-grade.
topics.story: A REST API is like a standardized restaurant menu. Resources are dishes (/api/orders, /api/users). HTTP methods are actions: GET=look at the menu, POST=place a new order, PUT=replace your entire order, PATCH=change just the drink in your order, DELETE=cancel. Status codes are the waiter's responses: 200=here's your food, 201=your order was placed, 404=we don't serve that, 400=your order is illegible, 409=that table is already taken, 500=the kitchen is on fire. Good API design means any new waiter (client developer) can understand the menu without a tutorial.
topics.analogy: Resources = nouns (/users, /orders, /products — never /getUser or /createOrder). HTTP verbs = actions. Idempotency = safe to call multiple times: DELETE /order/5 five times still results in order 5 being deleted. POST /orders creates a new order each time — not idempotent. 4xx = client's fault (fix your request). 5xx = server's fault (we messed up). 201 Created + Location header = we created it, here's where to find it. 422 Unprocessable Entity = valid syntax but fails business rules.
topics.memoryAnchor: GPPPD methods — GET (read, safe+idempotent), POST (create, neither), PUT (full replace, idempotent), PATCH (partial update, idempotent), DELETE (remove, idempotent). Idempotent test: call it 10 times = same result as 1 time? YES = idempotent. Safe test: does it change server state? GET only. Status code mnemonic: 2xx=Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 3xx=Redirect, 4xx=Your fault as client (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Validation Error), 5xx=Our fault as server (500 Internal Error, 503 Service Unavailable). URL design rule: NOUNS not verbs. /users not /getUsers. /orders/5/items not /getItemsForOrder?id=5.
topics.firstPrinciples: HTTP is the transport; REST constrains how you use it. Uniform interface: every resource has a stable URL, every action uses standard HTTP methods. Statelessness: each request contains all info needed (no server-side session per client). REST is not a standard — it's a style. HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) is often impractical; pragmatic REST is fine.
topics.bruteForce: RPC-style (old SOAP or bad REST): POST /api/getUserById, POST /api/createNewUser, POST /api/deleteUserById. All POSTs, all custom verb endpoints. No caching (POST isn't cached). No standard error format. Each endpoint is a unique contract — impossible to predict without documentation.
topics.optimizedApproach: Resource-oriented: GET /api/users?page=0&size=20, POST /api/users returns 201 with Location: /api/users/42. Consistent error format: {error: {code: 'USER_NOT_FOUND', message: '...', field: 'userId'}}. Pagination: {data: [...], meta: {page:0, size:20, total:340}}. Version in URL: /api/v1/... for major breaking changes.
topics.whenToUse: GET for reads (safe + idempotent). POST for creating new resources or non-idempotent operations. PUT for full replacement (client sends complete representation). PATCH for partial update (client sends only changed fields). DELETE for removal. Use query parameters for filtering, sorting, pagination. Use path params for resource identity. Never use GET with a request body. Always return 201+Location on successful POST. Use 204 No Content for successful DELETE with no body.

examples.title: RESTful Controller — Proper Status Codes & Error Handling
examples.explanation: ResponseEntity gives full control over status code, headers, and body. 201 Created + Location header is the correct POST response. 204 No Content on successful DELETE. Consistent error response shape (code + message) makes client error handling predictable. @Valid triggers Jakarta Bean Validation before the method runs — 400 is returned automatically if validation fails.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner API follows this pattern for /api/topics, /api/problems. Returning ResponseEntity<ApiResponse<T>> with a standard wrapper. GlobalExceptionHandler maps validation exceptions to 400, EntityNotFound to 404, DataIntegrityViolation to 409. The Location header on POST enables the client to immediately GET the new resource.

examples.title: Global Exception Handler & API Response Wrapper
examples.explanation: @RestControllerAdvice is a global exception handler — all controllers share it. @ExceptionHandler declares which exception type to catch. Returning standard ApiResponse<T> everywhere means the frontend always knows the shape of success and error responses. Field-level validation errors (fields map) let the frontend highlight specific form fields.
examples.realWorldUse: DevLearner GlobalExceptionHandler: maps JwtExpiredException → 401, AccessDeniedException → 403, ConstraintViolationException → 409, generic Exception → 500. Frontend React: response.data.success ? ... : show error.message. Consistent error codes like 'TOPIC_NOT_FOUND' let the frontend show user-friendly messages.

problems.title: Design URL Structure for a Blog API
problems.description: Design RESTful URL structure for a Blog API with: users, posts, comments (nested under posts), and tags. For each resource define: GET (list + single), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE. State HTTP status codes for each. Also design the error response JSON shape.
problems.hint1: Resources are nouns. Nested resources (comments under posts): /posts/{postId}/comments.
problems.hint2: Tags on a post: either /posts/{id}/tags or separate /tags with relationship.
problems.hint3: Create = 201 + Location. List = 200 + array. Get one = 200 or 404. Update = 200. Delete = 204.
problems.testCases: Follows resource-oriented URL design with correct HTTP methods and status codes

problems.title: Implement Idempotent PUT vs PATCH
problems.description: Implement a simple in-memory resource (Product with id, name, price, category). Show: PUT /products/{id} replaces the entire resource (null fields go to null). PATCH /products/{id} only updates provided fields. Demonstrate the difference with a test.
problems.hint1: PUT: replace entire object with what client sends (including nulls).
problems.hint2: PATCH: merge only non-null fields from request into existing object.
problems.hint3: After PUT with only {name:'New'}, price and category become null. After PATCH, they stay.
problems.testCases:
Original: 1|Laptop|Electronics|999.0
After PUT: 1|Laptop Pro|null|null
After PATCH: 1|Laptop|Electronics|1199.0

problems.title: HTTP Status Code Quiz — Map Scenarios to Codes
problems.description:
For each scenario, return the correct HTTP status code:
1. User submits form with missing required field
2. User requests /api/users/999 but no user with id 999
3. Unauthenticated request to /api/admin
4. Authenticated user tries to access another user's private data
5. Creating a user with email that already exists
6. Server-side NullPointerException
7. Rate limit exceeded
8. Successful resource creation
problems.hint1: 400 family = client errors. 500 family = server errors.
problems.hint2: 401 = not authenticated (no/invalid token). 403 = authenticated but no permission.
problems.hint3: 409 Conflict = already exists. 422 Unprocessable = valid syntax but business rule violation.
